choice1_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?
choice2_text: How can we assess and improve the external validity of comparative judgment tasks, i.e., the degree to which the CJ task elicits the preferences/beliefs that drive judges' behaviour/choices in other contexts?

choice1_text: Where there is a need for setting meaningful zero points or category thresholds for the latent measurement scale, what are good data collection and analysis practices for integrating scale calibration with comparative judgment?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice1_text: How should we determine the minimum number of pairwise comparisons needed for reliable scale construction?

choice1_text: Reliability = consensus? Researchers sometimes use reliability measures (SSR/SHR) as an indicator of consensus between the judges about some construct. Is this a valid inference?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice1_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?
